layout: default
---

# EX09: Data Analysis for Continuous Improvement

This page summarizes the **COMP110 Exercise 9** project: defining data utilities, analyzing anonymized course survey data, and interpreting results for continuous improvement.

## Summary of the analysis

Survey responses from **two CSV files** (`survey_izzi.csv` and `survey_alyssa.csv`) were loaded with `read_csv_rows`, converted to column form with `columnar`, combined with `concat`, and narrowed with `select`. Rows with empty Likert fields were filtered out so blanks were not treated as numbers. Numeric columns were analyzed with **pandas** and **seaborn**.

The research question was whether students who **more strongly agree that optional pre-lecture videos would help** (`pre_lecture_videos`) also report **stronger course recommendation** (`would_recommend`) and **higher perceived understanding** (`understanding`)—i.e., whether stated demand for pre-lecture prep aligns with measurable outcomes in the survey.

## Figures (bar, box, and violin)

The notebook produced **three seaborn visualizations** (not limited to scatter or line plots): a **bar plot** of mean recommendation by agreement level, a **box plot** of recommendation spread within each level, and a **violin plot** of understanding distributions across agreement levels. They are shown together below.

<img src="data:image/png;base64," alt="Three-panel figure: mean recommendation by pre-lecture video agreement, box plot of recommendation, and violin plot of understanding versus agreement." style="max-width: 100%; height: auto;" />

## Conclusions

**What I found:** After merging both survey files and restricting to rows with non-empty `pre_lecture_videos`, `would_recommend`, and `understanding`, the distributions show **modest positive alignment**: students who rate pre-lecture videos higher tend to report **slightly higher mean course recommendation** and **somewhat higher understanding**, and the correlation matrix is **positive but not overwhelming**—suggesting **demand for pre-lecture materials overlaps with goodwill and perceived comprehension**, but many other factors also drive outcomes.

**Support vs. refutation:** The evidence **partially supports** the idea that investing in optional pre-lecture videos aligns with student-reported value, but it **does not prove causation** (confounders include prior experience, section, and grade anxiety).

**Costs and trade-offs:** Producing reliable pre-lecture videos has **ongoing production cost for staff**, may **reduce attendance** if misused as a substitute for lecture, and can **widen inequity** if only some units receive high-quality videos.

**Extensions / future work:** Compare **video usage analytics** (if tracked) to outcomes; A/B test **one unit** with dense vs. light pre-reading; pair with **LSQ or quiz data** to see if prep shifts objective learning signals.

**If data were inconclusive:** Run a **targeted mid-semester poll** on which topics need primers and experiment with **optional short clips** for those topics only.
